Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
Relapse can happen during the quitting process. Instead of feeling discouraged, treat it as a learning experience and continue trying to quit.

Therapist: You've thought of a wonderful support system to help you stay on track with your goals, and I'm so proud of you for taking this important step.

Patient: It feels a little weird to be so serious about quitting, but talking to you has made it feel more doable, I guess. I'm starting to see it as a way to take care of my health, which is pretty important to me.

Therapist: You're feeling a bit more confident about quitting now that we've talked about your support system. Have you considered writing down your reasons for quitting and putting them somewhere you can easily see them when you're in situations that make you want to smoke?

Patient: That's not a bad idea. If I write down my reasons, like taking care of my asthma and being a better role model for my friends, maybe it'll help me remember why I'm quitting when I feel like having a cigarette.
